content: When you first start using std::time in Rust, the documentation makes it sound straightforward: you just call Duration::from_secs or from_millis to create a duration object. But as a senior engineer, I wish I had explained earlier that these constructors aren't magic; they are thin wrappers around integer arithmetic and overflow handling that you need to understand manually when dealing with high-frequency timers. The core data structure is std::time::Duration itself, which acts like a fixed-width signed 64-bit integer representing nanoseconds, but it exposes higher-level abstractions for convenience. When you call Duration::from_secs(10), the compiler generates code that multiplies the argument by one billion and wraps the result into the Duration field, but if your input exceeds the maximum representable nanoseconds—roughly 292 years—the function returns an error instead of silently clamping or wrapping, which prevents subtle bugs in production systems. You also need to respect the constants like Duration::ZERO for representing idle time and Duration::MAX as a theoretical upper bound, though in practice you rarely hit that limit unless you are modeling system-wide timeouts across decades. The real power comes from how these values interact with other Rust types: when converting between Duration and i64 using .as_secs() or .as_millis(), you lose precision because those operations truncate the nanosecond component, whereas as_secs_f64 preserves fractional seconds but introduces floating-point rounding errors if your duration spans many years. For subsecond resolution without loss of data, use .subsec_nanos() to extract the remaining nanoseconds after extracting whole seconds or milliseconds, which is critical when calculating elapsed time in high-performance networking stacks where microsecond or nanosecond precision matters more than wall-clock accuracy. Rust also provides literal syntax for creating Duration objects directly from string literals like "10s" or "300ms", but these are meant only for parsing input rather than constructing durations programmatically, so avoid using them in tight loops where performance counts; instead, stick to the arithmetic methods. Arithmetic on Duration follows standard signed integer rules: adding two Durations simply adds their nanosecond counts and carries over to seconds if necessary, while subtracting requires careful handling of negative results that represent overdue timeouts rather than forward progress. When designing systems around timing, remember that Duration is immutable once created unless you use the Copy trait, which allows it to be moved into larger structs without allocation overhead, making it ideal for passing time deltas through function calls or storing them in configuration files. Finally, don't confuse wall-clock time from chrono with relative elapsed time from std::time; while chrono provides Date and Time types for calendar operations, Duration is specifically designed for calculating differences between timestamps to determine how long something has been running since a particular epoch event.
